tldr: Headlines across 13 countries portray Trump's 80th birthday UFC cage fight on the White House lawn chiefly as a presidential power spectacle; a secondary strand in Spain and the UK signals bipartisan criticism, while the US progressive press and Pakistan are alone in framing the event as a symptom of sports-washing by an authoritarian leader.

evidence.quote: Trump to celebrate 80th birthday with cage fight
Trump's grand sporting season begins with UFC fights
Trump craves power at any cost. He told me so in four words
Trump to host UFC cage fight for 80th birthday
'The Claw' rises: White House Octagon ready
US-Regierung vertieft Kooperation mit Kampfsportliga UFC
Trump's grand sporting season begins with UFC fights
La Casa Blanca presenta ring de lucha para pelea de la UFC

frames.sub_frame: Bipartisan criticism of White House UFC

evidence.quote: UFC fight at the White House sparks a wave of criticism
Sportswashed: FIFA's Long Love Affair With Authoritarians
From cage fights to the White House, UFC marches

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only headline to deploy a classical political allegory — 'War and Circuses' invokes Juvenal's panem et circenses, framing the UFC birthday as imperial bread-and-games distraction from the Iran war. No other headline in the corpus attempts this register.

Only headline to invoke 'sportswashing' and to situate the UFC event within a pattern of authoritarian rulers using FIFA and international sport for legitimacy — shifting the frame from birthday spectacle to global political phenomenon.

Only headline to frame the event as evidence of authoritarian power-craving rather than festivity or controversy — 'Trump craves power at any cost' is the most adversarial framing in the corpus and the sole first-person attributed characterisation.

Only headline to lead with a diplomatic story entirely — 'Syrian president Ahmed al-Sharaa invited to US mid-June' — relegating the UFC cage fight to incidental body context. The headline conveys no UFC or birthday frame at all, placing pan_arab in silences.

Only non-English headline and the only outlet to foreground the government-industry partnership angle ('US-Regierung vertieft Kooperation mit Kampfsportliga UFC' — US government deepens cooperation with combat sports league UFC), framing the event as institutional entrenchment rather than birthday theatre.

Only headline to highlight a sponsorship irony — 'MAGA's least favorite beer is all over Trump's Octagon' — drawing attention to Bud Light's sponsorship of an event associated with the political base that previously boycotted the brand.

bottom_line: The headline layer frames Trump's White House UFC cage fight almost universally as a presidential power spectacle, with bipartisan disapproval visible only in Spain and the UK's Independent, and a sports-washing critique confined to US progressive and Pakistani press alone.
